policy_statement: Encourage local stakeholders to implement ISWM in priority intervention zones with a target of at least 50 voluntary ISWM projects supported via awareness campaign and grants.
mechanism_description: The City Council will encourage voluntary integrated rainwater management projects, supported by awareness campaigns and grants, to improve stormwater handling and reduce flood risk in priority zones.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall) → stormwater system overload/pluvial flooding → integrated rainwater management → ↓ runoff peak + ↑ infiltration/temporary storage → ↓ exposure and damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in activity/system: City capital projects in priority flood zones are designed to include integrated rainwater measures (e.g., permeable surfaces, bioswales, rain gardens, detention/retention, daylighting, storage/harvesting, nature-based or hybrid drainage).  
2) Mediating process: These measures increase on-site infiltration and/or temporary storage and slow conveyance, reducing peak discharge to sewers/waterways and lowering the likelihood of surcharging and surface ponding during intense rainfall.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced frequency/severity of pluvial flooding and associated damages/disruptions under current and future climate-driven precipitation extremes (improved urban flood resilience).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing flood risk and impacts from extreme rainfall by lowering peak stormwater runoff and local inundation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets stormwater runoff reduction to lower flood risk—i.e., reducing climate hazard impacts (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While integrated rainwater management can have co-benefits (urban cooling, biodiversity, modest energy/GHG effects via reduced pumping/treatment), the central and largest climate-relevant effect across such policies is flood risk reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented mainly to reduce potable water demand (via rainwater harvesting) it could shift toward Resource Efficiency; if framed primarily as carbon-sequestering green infrastructure it might touch Nature-Based Solutions; however, the stated mechanism explicitly prioritizes flood control zones and flood risk, keeping Adaptation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is “integrated rainwater management → reduced runoff/flood risk” principally functions to reduce exposure to extreme rainfall and flooding, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further labeling (e.g., grey vs green infrastructure, pluvial vs fluvial flood focus, co-benefits like heat mitigation) requires policy-specific evidence and is not set here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be dual-classified in other frameworks when rainwater capture is primarily for demand reduction (Resource Efficiency) or when green infrastructure is the main intervention (Nature-Based Solutions), but the dominant pathway described is flood risk reduction in priority flood zones (Adaptation).
key_indicators: "encourage"; "carried out on a voluntary basis"; "flood control awareness campaign"; "with the help of municipal and regional grants"; "At least 50 ... projects"; "priority intervention zones"; "integrated rainwater management system"
co_benefits: Improved water quality and reduced urban heat (via added green infrastructure); amenity/biodiversity gains
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a target ("At least 50") which could resemble target-setting, but the operative lever is grants/awareness to stimulate voluntary projects, so classified as an incentive program (with informational elements).
